subject: [PATCH v2 0/4] mm: fix reclaim storms in defrag_mode

reviews.author: Gregory Price
reviews.summary: reviewer suggested adding a comment to explain the WRITE/READ once mechanism, which is now detached from the main barrier() function
reviews.raw_body:
Might be worth a comment to explain what the WRITE/READ once is dealing
with here since it's now detached from the main barrier(), but otherwise

Reviewed-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>

reviews.author: Brendan Jackman
reviews.summary: Reviewer Brendan Jackman noted that his ALLOC_UNMAPPED series requires similar changes to make compaction run on orders above allocation order, and agrees with the patch's approach of using the capture_control for this purpose.
reviews.raw_body:
Just a note that I'm rebasing my ALLOC_UNMAPPED series onto this, per
the discusison here I have similar needs re making compaction run on
orders above the allocation order:

https://lore.kernel.org/all/17f2f1e7-36ee-4d90-af1c-1fbca6f94025@kernel.org/

I think the way you've done it (i.e. use the capture_control for the
"other" order) is good.

reviews.author: Andrew Morton
reviews.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton noted that the patch series cannot be backported to stable kernel due to dependencies between patches, and suggested either making the fix a standalone cc:stable hotfix or applying cc:stable to all four patches.
reviews.raw_body:
This cc:stable fix doesn't apply to current mainline because the
preceding non-cc:stable patches get in the way.

This means that the -stable maintainers will have to ask you to redo
the fix to make it backportable.  And you'll then be preparing a patch
which hasn't had standalone testing in mainline.  All very cumbersome.

So is it practical to make the fix come first, as a cc:stable hotfix so
we can upstream the other patches in the next merge window?

(An alternative would be to put cc:stable on all four patches.  That's
a bit abusive but we do it rarely).

Either way, I'll get these into mm.git for testing while we await
review on [4/4], thanks.

2026-07-22.patch_summary: This patch series addresses reclaim storms in defrag_mode by allowing non-movable requests to participate in pageblock production during direct reclaim and compaction, which helps prevent pressure spikes and swap storms. The patches make adjustments to the allocator and compaction code to enable this behavior, including changes to capture control and migration source scan restrictions. The approach taken is to modify the slowpath of the allocator to invoke direct reclaim and compaction with pageblock_order when non-movable requests are involved. This has been tested in production against affected workloads for several weeks and restores OOM kill rates to baseline levels.

reviews.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
reviews.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a zone is being compacted and its memory pressure is high, which can lead to reclaim storms

Reviewer Vlastimil expressed concerns that the patch series is too complex to be reviewed in a single patch and suggested splitting it into smaller patches, specifically mentioning dependencies between patches 2/4 and 3/4.

Reviewer Vlastimil expressed concern that the 'Stable-dep-off' tag is not necessary, suggesting it's up to the stable team to determine dependencies and backporting them.
reviews.raw_body:
+ Greg and Sasha

On 7/23/26 01:22, Andrew Morton wrote:

---

I think it can't come first, it's simply a complex fix that can't be done in
a single patch that would be reviewable. 3/4 is certainly a strict
dependency. I'm not sure if 2/4 as well. Perhaps 2/4 does deserve a Fixes:
tag and Cc: stable on its own, as it also fixes a defrag_mode deficiency.

---

I don't think it's necessary, the stable team are quite capable of figuring
out the deps and backporting them with a Stable-dep-off: tag. IIRC the
"fake" tagging is discouraged? Maybe they can clarify.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that patch 2/4 is a hard functional requirement for compaction to make progress, but expressed uncertainty about whether it deserves an independent Fixes: tag.

Author acknowledged a potential issue with automated backports in case patch 3/4 is lost or not applied, but believes it should still work with just the last patch.
reviews.raw_body:
Yes. Only 1/4 could be argued away.

2/4 actually came after 4/4 during development. Without it, compaction
cannot make sufficient forward progress on the promoted non-movable
requests. So it is a hard functional requirement.

Whether it deserves an independent Fixes: depends on how many
GFP_KERNEL pageblock_order requests we have :-) I'm guessing not many.

---

Yes, I *think* it should work with tagging just 4/4.

It won't apply cleanly or build without 3/4. So I'd assume if that
dependency is lost, the automated backport will bounce and I can send
a backport series by hand.

reviews.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Vlastimil noted that the patch series may cause issues in stable kernels if the fix for patch 4 is not backported along with patch 3, as leaving out patch 2 results in no conflicts during application.

reviewer noted that the patch series was not properly ordered, specifically mentioning a potential issue where patches 3 and 4 were sent out of order
reviews.raw_body:
So that's the tricky part then. If it's a hard functional requirement for
4/4 and yet leaving it out results in no conflicts when applying 3/4 and 4/4
(as it indeed seems so, based on my local test), then it's quite possible
stable folks will figure out themselves they need 3/4 as a stable-dep-off:
4/4 but won't backport 2/4, unless you act quickly enough on their mails.

---

The way I see if often happen is that Greg sends a mail that it bounced and
soon after Sasha replies with fixed up version/series. Which could very
easily be 3/4 + 4/4 without 2/4.

reviews.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
reviews.summary:
Gave Reviewed-by

reviewer noted that compaction_capture() disallows capturing <pageblock_order pages from MOVABLE pageblocks for !MOVABLE allocations, making premature capture unlikely to succeed but not incorrect
sentiment_signals: unlikely the capture will ever succeed
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah, a premature capture can't jeopardize the full-pageblock clearing
because compaction_capture() disallows capturing <pageblock_order page from
MOVABLE pageblock for !MOVABLE allocations.

It's thus unlikely the capture will ever succeed in these scenarios. But
it's not wrong.

reviews.author: Andrew Morton
reviews.summary: reviewer expressed confusion about the patch series and requested clarification on what 'stable-dep' means
reviews.raw_body:
What's that.

hp2:/usr/src/25> grep -ri stable-dep ../gitlog Documentation  
hp2:/usr/src/25>

reviews.author: Andrew Morton
reviews.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton requested that the Fixes tag be added to each patch, referencing the commit e3aa7df331bc.
reviews.raw_body:
My brain hurts.  I'll include

Fixes: e3aa7df331bc ("mm: page_alloc: defrag_mode")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>

in all four patches.

2026-07-23.patch_summary: This patch series addresses reclaim storms in defrag_mode by allowing non-movable requests to participate in pageblock production during direct reclaim and compaction, which helps prevent pressure spikes and swap storms. The patches make adjustments to the allocator and compaction code to enable this behavior, including changes to capture control and migration source scan restrictions. The approach taken is to modify the slowpath of the allocator to invoke direct reclaim and compaction with pageblock_order when non-movable requests are involved. This has been tested in production against affected workloads for several weeks and restores OOM kill rates to baseline levels.

reviews.author: Greg Kroah-Hartman
reviews.summary: Reviewer Greg Kroah-Hartman expressed appreciation for the patch author's efforts to ensure proper tagging, emphasizing that explicit tags prevent misunderstandings and ensure a smooth review process.
reviews.raw_body:
Thank you!  Don't make us "figure it out" as that way lies struggles and
time delays and the huge chance I get it wrong.  Tagging them all
ensures none of this happens and it all "just works".

thanks,

greg k-h
